name: How You See a Red Apple 🍎
scenario: You look at a red apple.
summary: Follow a single signal as it travels through the brain and turns light hitting your eyes into the urge to reach for the apple.

steps.title: Your eyes capture the light
steps.description: Photoreceptors in your eyes convert the apple's reflected light into nerve signals.
steps.what: Light reflecting off the apple hits your retina and is converted into electrical signals. (Simple: light from the apple goes into your eyes and becomes a brain message.)
steps.why: The brain can't process anything until the senses convert the outside world into signals it can read. (Simple: your brain can't see until your eyes send it a message.)
steps.analogy: Like a phone camera turning a scene into data.

steps.title: The thalamus relays the signal
steps.description: The thalamus forwards the visual signal to the visual cortex.
steps.what: The thalamus sorts the incoming signal and routes it to the visual cortex. (Simple: the sorter sends the message to the seeing part of the brain.)
steps.why: Routing sends each signal to the region built to handle it, instead of everywhere at once. (Simple: the message has to reach the right helper to make sense.)
steps.analogy: Like a mail sorter dropping a letter in the right mailbox.

steps.title: The visual cortex decodes features
steps.description: Colour, edges, shape, and depth are extracted from the signal.
steps.what: The visual cortex detects the object's colour, edges, and shape. (Simple: your brain works out that it's round and red.)
steps.why: Recognition is built from organised features, not from raw light. (Simple: knowing the shape and colour helps your brain guess what it is.)
steps.analogy: Like a game scanning an object to identify it.

steps.title: The hippocampus matches memory
steps.description: The image is compared with your stored knowledge of apples.
steps.what: The hippocampus links the current image to your stored concept of 'apple'. (Simple: your memory matches it with apples you've seen before.)
steps.why: Recognition depends on connecting new input with prior experience. (Simple: you recognise things by matching them to memories.)
steps.analogy: Like finding the matching card in a memory game.

steps.title: The amygdala adds emotional value
steps.description: The apple is tagged as safe, familiar, and rewarding.
steps.what: The amygdala attaches an emotional tag: this is safe and appealing. (Simple: your brain adds a feeling — this is good and I might want it.)
steps.why: Emotional tags help the brain prioritise what deserves attention. (Simple: feelings help you notice what matters.)
steps.analogy: Like putting a star sticker on your favourite thing.

steps.title: The prefrontal cortex decides
steps.description: Goals, hunger, and context are weighed to choose an action.
steps.what: The prefrontal cortex compares the evidence with your goals and chooses whether to act. (Simple: the thinker decides — grab it or leave it?)
steps.why: Flexible behaviour needs evidence weighed against goals before acting. (Simple: a smart choice means thinking before you act.)
steps.analogy: Like choosing the best move in a game.

steps.title: The motor cortex prepares movement
steps.description: A reach-and-grasp command is prepared for the muscles.
steps.what: The motor cortex sends movement commands to your arm and hand muscles. (Simple: your brain tells your hand to reach and grab.)
steps.why: A decision only has an effect once it becomes a body command. (Simple: a choice only helps if your body can do it.)
steps.analogy: Like sending instructions to a robot arm.

name: How You Learn Something New 🧠
scenario: You learn a new fact in class.
summary: See how a brand-new idea is encoded and consolidated into a lasting memory you can retrieve later.

steps.title: New information arrives
steps.description: A new concept enters through your senses.
steps.what: New words or images enter your senses and reach working awareness. (Simple: something new comes into your brain.)
steps.why: Learning begins only when information becomes available to attention. (Simple: learning starts when something new gets your attention.)
steps.analogy: Like opening a brand-new book.

steps.title: Attention holds it in focus
steps.description: The prefrontal cortex filters distraction and keeps the idea active.
steps.what: The prefrontal cortex sustains attention, holding the idea in working memory. (Simple: the thinker helps you focus and ignore distractions.)
steps.why: Unattended information rarely becomes durable memory. (Simple: you remember things much better when you really pay attention.)
steps.analogy: Like turning off the TV so you can study.

steps.title: The hippocampus encodes it
steps.description: Details are bound into a retrievable memory trace.
steps.what: The hippocampus binds the details into a single memory trace. (Simple: your brain packs the idea into a memory.)
steps.why: Binding lets the brain reconstruct the concept later. (Simple: saving it means you can find it again.)
steps.analogy: Like saving your progress in a video game.

steps.title: Associations form in the cortex
steps.description: The new memory links with existing knowledge networks.
steps.what: Cortical networks connect the new memory to related knowledge. (Simple: your new memory links up with old ones.)
steps.why: Connected memories are easier to store and recall. (Simple: connected ideas are much easier to remember.)
steps.analogy: Like adding a photo to an album full of related photos.

steps.title: Repetition strengthens the trace
steps.description: Repeated activation consolidates the memory.
steps.what: Each repetition strengthens the synaptic connections of the memory (consolidation). (Simple: practising makes the memory stronger.)
steps.why: Memory strength grows through repeated, coordinated firing of the same connections. (Simple: repeating something helps you keep it for a long time.)
steps.analogy: Like a path in the grass that gets clearer the more it's walked.

name: How You Remember a Friend's Name 👋
scenario: You want to remember your friend's name.
summary: Watch a goal-driven search reactivate a stored memory and return it, with a feeling of familiarity attached.

steps.title: The prefrontal cortex sets the goal
steps.description: It defines what you are trying to remember.
steps.what: The prefrontal cortex frames the retrieval goal: recall the name. (Simple: the thinker asks — what is my friend's name?)
steps.why: Retrieval is guided by your current goal and cues. (Simple: remembering starts with wanting to find something.)
steps.analogy: Like typing a query into a search bar.

steps.title: The hippocampus cues the search
steps.description: Face, voice, and context cues narrow the search.
steps.what: The hippocampus uses cues to reactivate related memory traces. (Simple: your brain uses clues like their face to find the memory.)
steps.why: Memories are reconstructed from partial cues, not replayed whole. (Simple: clues help your brain find the exact memory.)
steps.analogy: Like finding a contact by their photo.

steps.title: The stored pattern reactivates
steps.description: The name and its details become active again.
steps.what: The distributed memory pattern is reactivated across the cortex. (Simple: the saved memory wakes up.)
steps.why: Remembering means reactivating a stored pattern, not playing a recording. (Simple: remembering is waking up a saved memory.)
steps.analogy: Like reopening a saved game.

steps.title: Emotion signals familiarity
steps.description: A feeling of familiarity marks the memory as correct.
steps.what: The amygdala adds a familiarity signal that boosts your confidence. (Simple: a warm 'I know this' feeling appears.)
steps.why: Emotional tags help validate and prioritise recall. (Simple: that familiar feeling tells you the answer is right.)
steps.analogy: Like a green tick beside the right answer.

steps.title: The memory returns to use
steps.description: The recalled name enters conscious use.
steps.what: The prefrontal cortex brings the recalled name into conscious use. (Simple: the name is ready for you to use.)
steps.why: A memory becomes useful only when it can guide behaviour. (Simple: a memory helps once you can actually use it.)
steps.analogy: Like selecting a contact to send a message.

name: How You Decide to Cross the Road 🚦
scenario: You want to cross a busy road.
summary: See how sensory evidence, memory, emotion, and logic converge in the brain to produce one safe movement decision.

steps.title: Senses detect the traffic
steps.description: Eyes and ears pick up cars, movement, and sound.
steps.what: Your eyes and ears gather visual and auditory road cues. (Simple: you see and hear the cars.)
steps.why: Decisions need up-to-date evidence from the environment. (Simple: you need to know what's happening before you decide.)
steps.analogy: Like a car's sensors scanning the road.

steps.title: The thalamus splits the streams
steps.description: Visual and auditory signals are routed separately.
steps.what: The thalamus routes sight and sound to their own cortices. (Simple: the sorter sends pictures one way and sounds another.)
steps.why: Separating the streams lets each be analysed by a specialised region. (Simple: splitting them helps your brain understand each one.)
steps.analogy: Like sorting items into two different boxes.

steps.title: Motion and distance are estimated
steps.description: The visual cortex tracks how fast and near the cars are.
steps.what: The visual cortex estimates each car's speed, distance, and direction. (Simple: your brain works out where the cars are going.)
steps.why: Crossing safely depends on predicting moving objects. (Simple: you need to know if a car is coming.)
steps.analogy: Like a motion tracker predicting a path.

steps.title: Sound adds a warning
steps.description: The auditory cortex detects horns and engine direction.
steps.what: The auditory cortex interprets horns and engine noise as warnings. (Simple: you hear a honk that warns you.)
steps.why: Hearing can reveal threats outside your field of view. (Simple: your ears catch danger your eyes miss.)
steps.analogy: Like an alert catching something off-screen.

steps.title: Memory recalls the rules
steps.description: Stored road rules and past experience are retrieved.
steps.what: The hippocampus retrieves learned road rules and past experiences. (Simple: you remember to look both ways.)
steps.why: Prior learning helps predict danger and the right response. (Simple: what you learned before keeps you safe now.)
steps.analogy: Like checking the rules of a game.

steps.title: The amygdala assesses risk
steps.description: Danger and urgency are flagged.
steps.what: The amygdala marks the situation as low, uncertain, or high risk. (Simple: the alarm checks if it's dangerous.)
steps.why: Emotion helps prioritise safety-critical decisions. (Simple: feeling the danger helps you be careful.)
steps.analogy: Like a warning light on a dashboard.

steps.title: Logic weighs the options
steps.description: Risk, timing, rules, and goal are compared.
steps.what: The prefrontal cortex weighs risk, timing, and goal to choose wait or cross. (Simple: the thinker decides — wait or go.)
steps.why: Controlled decisions balance urgency against long-term safety. (Simple: a good choice keeps you safe and gets you across.)
steps.analogy: Like a planner comparing safety and time.

steps.title: The motor cortex issues the command
steps.description: A step-forward or stay-still command is prepared.
steps.what: The motor cortex prepares the command to step forward or to stay put. (Simple: your brain tells your legs to go or stop.)
steps.why: A decision must become a concrete body command to have any effect. (Simple: your choice only works when your body follows it.)
steps.analogy: Like sending the final go signal to a controller.
